item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is sad but the world is a little better because it has this story in it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something awfully poignant about an SCP simply dying of natural causes. It gives this article a nice sense of realness to it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: Akio Tanikawa
Licence: CC BY-SA 2.5

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something about this that I just really like. I was particularly struck by that last interview and the feeling that, for the spider, this revelation was similar to that felt by humans learning about the supernatural for the first time — that realization that there's something so much bigger and more powerful than you are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta say in resent months this Scp as become my number one. Honestly made an account just to upvote ha, but I also had some questions I've been burning to ask in that time.

I noted before you stated you changed part way to have him died instead of still living, but had you not changed that would you have added how he was captured and cantained?

Not only so but I find myself very curious on missed out opportunity of certain reactions such as his very first where he found someone to speak with.

I also wondered if they ever did tests? There are quite a few such as a mirror test to see if he could recoize himself or possibly taught to, maybe even small matching tests for memory.

Which speaking of you also mentioned he had a poor memory, did they ever see how poor it was? In the article he always seemed to recall the doctor and concidered them a friend, but was there ever I time the doctor couldn't show up for the daily chats and he question where they went or was the little guy just content with the next voice, though again he did seem to recoize he talked to multiple people with comments like "not like you guys do"

Oh geez this is long, sorry in advance. But again this has become a favorite of mine as I've moved away the crueler and scary side of the Scp wiki to enjoy these more cute or sad Scp's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: poor little fella

item.title: Sorrowful
item.content:encoded: :( Poooooor baby! I adore spiders and would have loved the chance to talk to one. I wonder if 1470 got exposed to some other SCP that allowed it to gain sentience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just gonna leave this here with my upvote: I am touched by a spider. G'day, mates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this SCP article. It showed how desperately the spider wanted to communicate with its own kind, including his mate, and realizing that he could talk telepathically to humans. While I was reading it, I was happy he was able to communicate with a doctor and express how he felt about his life, especially with an australian accent. After I finished, it made me tear up from how tragic it became in the end. You will be missed, SCP-1470.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, Australia gets a bit of love :D

Upvoted for patriotism!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made me feel bad about a spider. I love you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh my god the feels
take my +1, i love this guy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had a pet spider once, so this one hit me especially hard. Screw you for making me have the spider feels. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a total arachnophobe, and you made me shed tears for a spider… Well done. And now I'll never be able to kill a spider. One of the best SCPs I've read in a long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's too bad they never thought to introduce him to the Communist spiders before he died…

(^the above is a possible contender for the oddest statement I have ever written.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is fucking brilliant in so many ways upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always worried i'll hate 300+ scp's because hype train, but this one was good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How am I only just now reading this. You made me sad for a spider. As an avowed arachnophobe, I am pleasantly surprised and slightly baffled by this developmet. I upvoted this so hard I think I broke the button.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should have had the talking spider and the talking seaslug together for a conversation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A thought just occurred to me. In 'At the Mountains of Madness', when they discover the ice-encased bodies(or was it just one?) an Elder Thing. The scientists there, after having thawed the creature out, were about to dissect it. They thought it was very, very much dead, and had absolutely no reason to question that. Well, as they're beginning, it starts, IIRC, screaming of some sort, out of pain, fear, ect. Lovecraft made it very clear that this thing was not some unspeakable horror that feeds on fear and despair. Oh screw it, I'm terrible at trying to explain this, I'll just post the whole passage-

Dyer(Lovecraft's author avatar) - "Alter all, they were not evil things of their kind. They were the men of another age and another order of being. Nature had played a hellish jest on them—as it will on any others that human madness, callousness, or cruelty may hereafter dig up in that hideously dead or sleeping polar waste—and this was their tragic homecoming. They had not been even savages-for what indeed had they done? That awful awakening in the cold of an unknown epoch—perhaps an attack by the furry, frantically barking quadrupeds, and a dazed defense against them and the equally frantic white simians with the queer wrappings and paraphernalia … poor Lake, poor Gedney… and poor Old Ones! Scientists to the last—what had they done that we would not have done in their place? God, what intelligence and persistence! What a facing of the incredible, just as those carven kinsmen and forbears had faced things only a little less incredible! Radiates, vegetables, monstrosities, star spawn — whatever they had been, they were men!"

This is not a spider. This is a fellow sapient in a form simply different from ours, who was as a brother to us. He had dreams, hopes, likes & dislikes. A sense of humour. He felt both love and heartache. And when he knew his time was coming to an end he faced it with dignity. In many ways, he's akin to a terrestrial 163.

Articles like this(1470, 163, 1850 and others like them) are, in my opinion, some of the most important. The Foundation does many horrible things, and claims to only be doing them for the greater good of mankind. Well, actions speak louder than words. And this shows us that they genuinely are trying to be as humane, understanding and accommodating as possible to (certain)SCPs who, if you think about it, are genuine victims. No one asks to be born with whatever they happen to have. As so long as they cooperate, we'll do what we can to turn their jail into a home*.

*I don't mean giving them access to every thing, giving them absurdly luxurious cells or things of that nature. But enough so that they're comfortable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the above post. So much. I hate spiders so damn much, but I wish this one didn't die…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made me cry a little bit. For a SPIDER. +1. And I do love how we were the Eldritch Horrors; not because we were aweful or alien or nightmarish, all of which I've seen done, but just because we're so damned BIG compared to the little guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't top that. Pretty much covered everything.

Going to submit to The Volgun, but that's all I got to add.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't upvoted this yet? Goddamn it this is becoming a frustrating trend. This article is amazing. Eliciting a fear-response or revulsion is pretty simple, all things considered. It's primal. But to take something our species sees as being wrong in every possible way(too many eyes, too many legs, builds murder-traps, strangely intelligent) and evoke sympathy, endearment and love as if it were one of us, a brother-sentient with fears, hopes, dreams and imagination? Without it coming across as cheesy or forced? That's something special right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredible. Most of the "emotional" SCPs that everyone cries and praises over makes me roll my eyes but this, this is exactly how it should be done. No tearjerkers, no messages from the grave, just the humanity displayed by a being 6mm long coming to terms with his existence. +1, you deserve it.

item.title: SCP-1470: Talking Spider
item.content:encoded: I did not know it was possible to love an SCP subject before. Now I do. v_v

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea of us being the unknown, seen through another's eyes. Fantastic job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That poor spider :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very touching. I felt for a spider.

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: RIP, Spiderbro. I hate spiders but I can't help but love this little guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's saaaaaaaad! ;_;

+1

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Spiderbro will be in all our hearts.

item.title: Re: +201
item.content:encoded: I forgot to upvote ;_;

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Never forget spiderbro.
Talking animals

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I do. I think it is my favorite Sorts SCP and I have enthusiastically upvoted. I would just like to know how the Foundation found this little guy? He is SO small and Queensland is SO big, and he lived for such a relatively short period of time…

Regardless of the way he was discovered, I feel lucky to have read his story. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. Exactly this.

Except for the part about saying "Well done" again, coz I don't think I've said it yet. So. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorts, you have all my respect for made me cry for a spider. Also that part when He realizes what the doctor was..

Completely amazing. Also, as a bonus, you showed me the nice side of the foundation.

+1 is not enough, but it's a white rose for that spider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hurt. This hurts. Oh god, sapient animals with brutally short life spans murder me. I love spiders at the worst of times- they're such fascinating little things, shrewd problem solvers and tiny casual factories of what is possibly the most useful and interesting substance in the world- and the prospect of this lonely little guy, realizing at the end how terribly alone he always really was and- help me- actually sort of coping with it, is just killing me. There's so much subtext here that I'm not even sure if I'm grateful that he died or horribly, horribly sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the entry that finally made me really want to join this website. It's a wonderfully written article that made me feel attachment to a spider, which otherwise I am not very fond of. This entry touched me and I had to be able to upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeash, this is one of the most moving things on this site. Cudos to you, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My feels. That is really all there is to say on the matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no hurtz why i hate spiders but i am crying ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I closed the window before hitting post last time, but thought I'd leave this here for you:

http://imgur.com/gallery/D5Ieu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent article; I particularly like the regional dialect, for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. It's a surprisingly emotional story, and I like how it's a good example of an intelligent SCP that isn't treated completely dispassionately (or cruelly) by the Foundation. There's almost a tone of reverence in the way he was described as being 'laid to rest'. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorts, stop, I am weeping tears for the spider. ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short answer is 1470 wasn't very smart. He seemed that way due to the very translation concept, but his worldview was microscopic and his memory was tiny. He was aware of larger animals and to get away from them, but a researcher's hand was basically "a big predator" — and he didn't know there was an arm attached to that hand and something even bigger beyond that. The researchers were just the big weird shapes that moved in the distance which were to be avoided at all costs because big weird shapes were not good things. He thought that the people talking to him were other spiders who were hiding from him, only spiders could possibly be intelligent after all, except for those savage ones that are only good for eating.

It wasn't until he was about to die that he even started thinking about other things, and that was when he had the epiphany and realized that those other voices in his head were actually coming from the giant blurry shapes from beyond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think what makes this work is the automatic concept translation, palps=arms etc.
It's a completly plausible mechanism in the context of telepathy, and it makes it perfectly plausible that 1470 could make such a big oversight. If he got any self-image impressions from the researchers, they would be translated into spider equivalents, so of course he would think they were fellow spiders.
A tiny hole in the plot, where did 1470 think these females were coming from? If he didn't realize that there was an outside to the terarium, any statements like "we're going to add a female to your enclosure." would just seem like crazy gibberish to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You sir, just used logic to win a potential argument on the internet. Congratulations. (even in the foundation that is impressive)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Spoida's a good job, mate!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hear the spider's voice as the Sniper from Team Fortress 2. Is that weird?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit I usually have an instinctual reaction to kill any spiders I find because they're… they're disgusting…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spiders are gross and mean, and they're also awesome and amazing. They trap and devour alive things which spend the last few minutes/hours of its life in the relative equivalent of abject fear; they do this with a material that's pound-for-pound more resilient than virtually any material mass-produced by humans. They have SO MANY EYES; those eyes let them see the world in a way that would make a human's brain explode. The females of many species eat their mates; they then typically defend their egg clutches as ferociously as possible, and there are some species that, upon hatching, will lay down and let their own children devour them. Also, they eat the bajeebus out of other, more pestilent species.

The only time I squash a spider is if it scares the crap out of me (like when they like to rappel down on a string directly between my face and the monitor, or are on a pillow near my head when I wake up) or if it's one of those annoying little jumping spiders that were infesting the office a year or two ago, because seriously, fuck those things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to say well done once again.
This little bugger makes me all mushy, and normally, I fucking hate spiders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this is why I tell my friends not to kill spiders +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dammit, this is sad as hell. Upvote for telepathic Australian spider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's implied by the date containment procedures changed that they've been keeping tabs on the offspring for years, and they're just ordinary spiders. They could just exterminate the lot of them and call it a day, but this is one of those cases where the researchers are being a little sentimental. I could change that, but it would conflict with the casket they set out and ruin the tone.

I swear I totally didn't mean to write a "sympathize with a spider" story, I guess I'm a nicer person than I try to be. :( I'm going to go write some mini tale where he spends three days stalking another spider to murder and devour it. Spiders are gross and mean.
